header.title: Queue
header.description: A queue stores elements in First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order — elements are enqueued at the rear and dequeued from the front. Front and rear pointers give O(1) access to both ends, making all core operations constant time.

howItWorks.title: How queues work
items: A queue maintains two pointers: front (the next element to dequeue) and rear (where the next enqueue goes). Both operations are O(1) — no scanning, no shifting.
Enqueue writes the new element at rear+1 and advances the rear pointer. Dequeue reads from front and advances the front pointer. Neither operation touches any other element.
In a naive array implementation, dequeueing leaves a gap at the front — unused space grows over time. A circular buffer solves this by wrapping the front and rear pointers using modulo arithmetic.
A priority queue breaks FIFO order — elements leave based on a priority key, not arrival time. It is implemented as a heap, making enqueue and dequeue O(log n) instead of O(1).
A deque (double-ended queue) allows enqueue and dequeue at both ends in O(1) — it generalises both stacks and queues.
Because queues process elements in arrival order, they are the natural data structure for BFS, task scheduling, and any system where fairness (first come, first served) matters.

variants.label: Simple Queue (FIFO)
variants.description: The foundational queue — elements enter at the rear and leave from the front in strict arrival order. All core operations are O(1) with front and rear pointers.
characteristics: FIFO guarantee — first enqueued is always first dequeued
O(1) enqueue and dequeue — no shifting, just pointer advances
Naive array impl wastes front space — circular buffer fixes this
Linked list impl: O(1) with head (front) and tail (rear) pointers

variants.label: Circular Queue (Ring Buffer)
variants.description: A fixed-size queue where the rear pointer wraps around to the front of the array using modulo arithmetic. Reuses freed slots — no wasted memory and no array copying.
characteristics: Fixed capacity — full when (rear+1) % cap === front
rear = (rear+1) % cap and front = (front+1) % cap on each op
Eliminates the wasted-space problem of naive array queues
Used in OS ring buffers, network packet buffers, audio streaming

variants.label: Deque (Double-Ended Queue)
variants.description: Generalises both stack and queue — elements can be added or removed from either end in O(1). Implemented as a doubly linked list or circular buffer.
characteristics: O(1) at both ends — pushFront, pushRear, popFront, popRear
Implements stack (use one end) and queue (use both ends)
Python collections.deque, Java ArrayDeque, C++ std::deque
Sliding window maximum algorithm uses a monotonic deque

variants.label: Priority Queue (Heap-backed)
variants.structure:
enqueue(val, priority) → heap insert O(log n)
dequeue() → always removes min/max priority element
variants.description: Elements leave in priority order, not arrival order. Backed by a min-heap or max-heap — enqueue and dequeue are O(log n), peek is O(1).
characteristics: Dequeue always returns highest-priority (min or max) element
O(log n) enqueue/dequeue — heap property maintained via sift-up/down
O(1) peek — top of heap is always the priority extremum
Build from n elements in O(n) via heapify — faster than n inserts

files.code:
# ─── Queue in Python ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# collections.deque is a doubly-linked list — O(1) append and popleft.
# Use it directly for queues and deques.
# queue.Queue is thread-safe with blocking for producer-consumer patterns.
# heapq gives a min-priority queue.

from collections import deque
import heapq

# ── Simple queue (FIFO) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
q = deque()
q.append(10)       # O(1) enqueue at rear
q.append(20)
q.append(30)
front = q.popleft()   # O(1) dequeue from front → 10
peek  = q[
    0
]          # O(1) peek front → 20
empty = len(q) == 0   # O(1) isEmpty

# ── Deque (double-ended) ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
dq = deque()
dq.append(10)         # add to right (rear)
dq.appendleft(5)      # add to left (front)
dq.pop()              # remove from right
dq.popleft()          # remove from left

# ── Circular buffer (fixed capacity) ─────────────────────────────────────
class CircularQueue:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.buf   = [None
] * capacity
        self.cap   = capacity
        self.front = 0
        self.rear  = -1
        self.count = 0

    def enqueue(self, value):
        if self.count == self.cap: raise OverflowError('Full')
        self.rear = (self.rear + 1) % self.cap   # wrap around
        self.buf[self.rear
] = value
        self.count += 1

    def dequeue(self):
        if self.count == 0: raise IndexError('Empty')
        val = self.buf[self.front
]
        self.front = (self.front + 1) % self.cap  # wrap around
        self.count -= 1
        return val

    def peek(self):    return self.buf[self.front
]
    def is_empty(self):return self.count == 0
    def is_full(self): return self.count == self.cap

# ── Priority queue using heapq (min-heap) ────────────────────────────────
pq = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (1, 'high priority'))   # (priority, value)
heapq.heappush(pq, (3, 'low priority'))
heapq.heappush(pq, (2, 'medium priority'))
priority, value = heapq.heappop(pq)        # → (1, 'high priority')

footerAlerts.title: When queues shine
items: BFS traversal — a queue gives natural level-by-level expansion, guaranteeing shortest path in unweighted graphs.
Producer-consumer systems — queues decouple producers and consumers, letting each run at its own pace.
Fair scheduling — FIFO ensures no task is starved; every request is served in arrival order.
Sliding window — a deque (monotonic queue) solves maximum/minimum in a sliding window in O(n).

footerAlerts.title: When to consider alternatives
items: Priority ordering needed → use a priority queue (heap-backed, O(log n) ops).
Random access by index → use an array or deque with indexing.
Last-in first-out needed → use a stack instead.
Thread-safe concurrent access → use BlockingQueue (Java) or asyncio.Queue (Python).

hints: Always clarify whether a circular buffer or linked list is expected — the interviewer may want the space-efficient version.
For BFS: queue.offer(root) → while (!queue.isEmpty()) { node = queue.poll(); process; enqueue children }.
Priority queue edge cases: ties in priority (use insertion order as tiebreaker), empty dequeue, single-element heap.
